title: Why the A.I. Backlash Turned Violent in America

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: All right everybody, welcome back to this week in startups. It's Friday that means Lon Harris is here with me and at the end of the show we will do off duty with Lon. Everybody loves that segment where Lon takes us off duty and we share what we're buying, what we're consuming, what we're watching.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Let me just start the show by saying I love Plaud. I'm using my Plaud pin right now to record this very conversation. Why?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I have mine too, did you activate it yet?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah no it's running. It's been - my Plaud is - my Plaud goes all day.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yes, and I have mine running now and I just, you know, it's got the red light so I'm being privacy first and, you know, I only use it when people know I'm using it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: But this is the interesting thing. You could wear the Plaud pin like you're wearing it, or you can wear it on your wrist. Another great way to wear it. And what I do with this is when I'm hiking around the ranch or I have a moment, Plaud, take a memo, action item, we need to move two of our researchers into the paid newsletter that we're doing. Please have Lon pick his number one and two even though that's super uncomfortable to ask in front of everybody, the one and two, which is why you didn't answer it.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I sent you my one and two picks privately. I don't like to pick favorites in front of people.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: See, no, I like to create a little bit of like, who's ready for this? Anyway, that's my management style, but Plaud is amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And what it does is it creates a summary and then you can share that summary. Then they have like 20 different - I don't know, maybe it's hundreds, but I have like 20 really nice templates I use, like full accurate transcript. It learns who Lon is in my life. It learns who Alex is. It knows them, so then for all time forward I don't have to mention that. It creates a mind map of the conversation we have when we have a strategy meeting. All this great stuff and then one click share. Why is it better than using your phone? Oh my god, your phone's microphone stinks. And you have to though clumsily go through your phone as opposed to beep, one button, you're ready to go.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's the contextual - it understands the conversation and really thinks about how to organize it for you. That's the really...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: What's the offer? What's the offer? We have an offer?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: We do have an offer, Jason, of course. So you could check it out at plaud.ai/twist and if you use the code twist, they're going to give you 10% off your new Plaud pin. That's P-L-A-U-D.ai/twist.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: And please, if you do have a good experience with it, share it on social and tell everybody you found out about it on this week in startups on Twist.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, definitely appreciate it, please.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: All right, let's get started. We did our first thank you to our sponsors, but we've got a really great first guest. I'm excited about this one.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I am excited. I saw - I saw some of their tweets going by, I read a little bit about the startup and I immediately pinged the team like, let's - let's get these guys on. I want - I want to learn more about this. So we've got Matt Galich, he's the CEO and co-founder of AstroForge. They are a mining startup, they're developing spacecraft to go prospect and extract platinum group metals or PGMs from near-Earth asteroids.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Jason, that's the plan.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: So literally science fiction is reality. Matt, welcome back. Have you been on the program before, Matt, or is this your first time?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Hey, I talked to you maybe three years ago when we first launched before we had ever launched our first spacecraft.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so congratulations. Where are you at with mining asteroids?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Look, this is a really hard thing to do. I mean, we are the first commercial company that really wants to go outside of the Earth-Moon system and explore the solar system. Right? It's a totally big swing on space. We've seen a lot of venture dollars go into space, a lot of companies start up into space, and we're kind of that next step in what you can go do. Right now we're building Deep Space 2. It's our next spacecraft. That's a 200-kilogram spacecraft that'll launch later this year on a Falcon 9 that goes to the moon, and it'll attempt to go out and potentially land on an asteroid.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Wow, I want to jump in here. Look, I was doing a little research at this. When you say land on an asteroid, are we talking about like the rocket like chip comes down like in the sci-fi and it lands on the surface? It's more... is there... it's sort of like a docking procedure like with the International Space Station, correct?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: It's much more like a docking procedure. I mean, we have to kind of like... what the movies tell you and what's actually out there are two very different things, right? We go to what are called near-Earth asteroids. They're pretty close to the planet. The one we're targeting right now is about 10 million miles away. And these asteroids are around 200 meters in diameter. So in terms of space, these are very, very small things in space that we want to go land on. Now, we're also looking for a very specific type of asteroid. We're looking for an asteroid called a metal asteroid. And because of that, these asteroids are about 70 percent iron, somewhere in that range, iron nickel, which means they're magnetic. So the way we land on them...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Individual threats against people who support data centers, but also just that there is a lot of political resistance forming in opposition to these data centers by people who I think think that this sort of boom is bad and at least that they don't want it taking place in their neighborhood.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So describe some of this resistance.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: So the state of Maine recently passed a temporary moratorium that would ban data centers larger than 20 megawatts until November 2027. There's a suburb of Milwaukee, Wisconsin, Port Washington, which is going to be the home of one of these big OpenAI Oracle Stargate data centers. That town recently voted overwhelmingly in favor of restricting the building of future data centers, basically you have to get voter approval before you do any of these things. Then there's also similar efforts going on in places like Ohio, Missouri, Indiana, Georgia, North Carolina. And there's also this big federal data center moratorium that has been proposed by Bernie Sanders in the Senate and AOC in the House that would basically put a national moratorium on the construction of data centers. So I think data centers are kind of where this is becoming hottest fastest, but I do think we are seeing also these individual threats on the executives and leaders of tech companies.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: these big AI companies by people who just do not think this is headed in a good direction.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yes, and on that point, I want you to describe a little bit some of the broader backdrop here. Because over the past several weeks, I have seen survey after survey that just says in one way or another Americans do not believe that AI is likely to be a positive in their lives. So tell us about some of that data.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: What you see is kind of a slow turn against the AI industry over the course of really the last year or two. So there's a new report out from Stanford this week, the 2024 version of their AI index, which sort of catalogs various trends in the AI industry. And basically, their takeaway was that in the US, people have very low trust in not only AI, but on the question of whether their own government can regulate AI in a responsible way. The global average on that question was 54% of like, do you trust your own government to responsibly regulate AI? In the US, that's only 31%. The data is a little fuzzier in some other studies. There was a Pew study earlier this year that showed that people's attitudes in the US are more negative than positive when it comes to data centers' impact on the environment, home energy costs, and quality of life nearby. But that more Americans view the economic effects of AI as being potentially more positive. So it's a little fuzzy, sort of depending on how you slice the data and how you ask the questions. But I think it's fair to say that like most polls and surveys of public sentiment around AI have shown that people are getting more concerned as these systems get more powerful.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So let's talk about why we think this turn has happened so dramatically. I think we have a few possible explanations. And I want to start by one that was offered by Sam Altman, who wrote a very personal post on his blog. He put as a lead image a photo of his husband and his baby. And in this post, Sam talks about the story that was in the New Yorker the week previously, which we discussed on the most recent episode of Hard Fork. And one of the things that Sam writes is, words have power too. There was an incendiary article about me a few days ago. Someone said to me yesterday they thought it was coming at a time of great anxiety about AI and that it made things more dangerous for me. I brushed it aside. Now I'm awake in the middle of the night and pissed. So what do you make of the idea, Kevin, that a reason for the sort of negative sentiment against the AI companies and

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: fixes all of your ums and all of the punctuation, all of the filler and unnecessary words being removed in real time. Whisperflow is already available everywhere. When I'm using it in Superhuman, Gmail, Slack, Google Docs, I use it everywhere. The messages sound like I wrote them. We're moving into a world

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: ...where agents are going to make us a hundred times more efficient. Now you add Whisperflow making you five, 10, 20 times more efficient. Visit whisperflow.ai/twist to get started for free today. This product's amazing. That's W-I-S-P-R-F-L-O-W.ai/twist. I've been using this product for six months. It is extraordinary.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I'd be lying to you if I said I was 100% confident we can do this. I'm a founder with a thesis that we can get out into the universe for $10 million and go mine an asteroid. And as soon as I'm 100% confident, that means I'm not paying attention to all the things that can go wrong. Like, this is a very technical company and a very technical job as CEO to go run and figure out if we can do this. And that's kind of what we spend all our time doing, J-Cal. I mean, like, it is really from, I think Elon showed us the way here of, be one of the best engineers on the team that can go develop these products. You have to know this in and out of how we're going to go do this. And that's what I spend all my time doing.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Remind us again, when does this mining mission occur? And are we going to get to watch it live? I mean, one of the things I've been amazed over the years is that Elon has really high-fidelity video from all of these, you know, different missions now, like even the multiple stages. I'm not sure how he does that. I'm assuming Starlink is involved. I know they have, like, helicopters and drones, you know, doing the low earth or doing the atmosphere video. But take us into bringing the world into watching you do this. Is that a key part of this? And is that a key part of why I think Elon's success with SpaceX is bringing humanity along for the ride through live streaming?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I'm going to be honest with you, I wish we could live stream at the asteroid. The answer is we cannot. When we're at the asteroid and the way we save money is very simple, we lower our data rate dramatically. So at the asteroid, the max we're talking to spacecraft at is about 400 bits per second.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Easy to me, because it's like at this point we're not even really talking about the rhetoric, we're talking about the actual technology and the material effect it's already starting to have on people's lives.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, see, I would ask you about this because I feel like there's a certain bind here that these companies and their leaders are in when it comes to talking about some of the scarier possible outcomes of AI. I think a lot of them watched the social media CEOs claim that their technologies during the last decade would produce nothing but good for the world, right? It was going to connect everyone, it was going to be this, you know, glorious utopia. And I think a lot of them took the lesson from that that, well, we have to be upfront if we think the thing that we're building has some risk attached to it, like we should be open and honest about that and not sugarcoat it. So I, I see them as kind of being stuck here because if they did what, you know, what you are suggesting maybe they should have done and...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: And we had a failure right away. One of the failures that happened was the solar panels did not deploy.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Talk about the risks that they see and they're honest about their fears, um, then they're accused of being doomers who only want to escalate the rhetoric and stir things up and I just like how do you think they should square that circle? So I think that there is...

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: There is a third path forward here, which is to essentially just try to work with the government and put more pressure on them to put into place systems that would regulate the companies to mitigate the harms caused by their products. And so far, we have actually seen the opposite, particularly in the case of OpenAI, right? As we have seen more and more regulations get proposed in state houses around the country, OpenAI is going around trying to prevent those bills from being passed into law. So I think this is really, really important, right? Because like the way that we like solve problems caused by companies in a democracy is that we regulate them. And when the companies themselves are out there lobbying against regulation, I think people find that more and more infuriated.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: All right, so we don't seem to think that journalism is the reason that people are so upset with the AI companies. Let me propose a second answer: economic worries. I feel like when you and I are out there talking to most folks, to the extent that people have a concrete near-term worry about AI, it is that it is either going to totally replace their job or it is just going to make the job that they have now horrible. How does that sound as an explanation to you?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I am sort of more in line with this view of things that sort of in the same way that all politics is local, I think that all AI politics or most AI politics ultimately comes down to people just sort of looking at a technology and thinking what will this do to me and my ability to continue to live my life and support my family and, you know, retire comfortably? Like I think people have a lot of fears about this stuff. And and that is, I think, a bigger part of the sort of data center opposition. I don't think a lot of the people opposing data centers are worried about existential risk. I think they're mostly focused on like this thing seems super annoying and maybe it's going to take my job and pollute my environment. And I think people are saying, wait a minute, we're supposed to be rooting for this? Like why would I root for something that might make it harder for me to put food on the table?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I really do think that is the skeleton key that unlocks some vast portion of the entire AI debate, right? And it doesn't seem to be something that so far any of the AI companies have really had an answer for. Now, if you sort of, you know, keep them up late at night and have one of these like dorm room conversations, they will describe for you visions of fully automated luxury communism where you don't have to have a job anymore and neither does anyone in your family and, you know, all of your meals will be 3D printed for you and it won't cost you a cent. And I think that just seems so implausible to people that it's impossible to build any kind of political constituency around it today, even if they're gonna try to sell that case a little bit harder in the future, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, I think this is the biggest cultural disconnect between the San Francisco Silicon Valley AI bubble and the rest of the country. I think people here, you know, many of the people I talk to, they are excited about a period of rapid technological...

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: logical change. That is what excites them, they're motivated by making it happen, they think, you know, ultimately this will be a good thing for society. I think most people don't think like that. They don't think, I want to live through a period of unprecedented technological change in which the world becomes unrecognizable to me. And I think there are a lot of people trying to send that message by opposing data centers, but I don't think it's really sunk in at the AI companies or to the people running them that most people want stability in their lives. They want to be able to plan for their futures, and when people from Silicon Valley show up and say, hey, we've got this amazing new technology, and by the way, it might take away your job and there's nothing you can do about it, um, I think that naturally breeds some fear and resentment.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So this gets into the third explanation for what I think is going on here, which I want to put under the broad label of anti-elitism, right? This AI moment that we're living through is a top-down moment. It did not rise up from the grassroots from a bunch of nerds getting together in their garages and training frontier models. It was a small group of really smart people who were able to get access to massive amounts of capital from the elites on our society and they are now mounting this effort to build it very quickly, deploy it very quickly without a lot of guardrails. And so I think when the average person looks at this, they think, not only did I not ask for this, but I have no meaningful control over it, right? Um, and so I just think that that is a big reason that you're seeing people so furious because I think particularly on the left, this just looks like a mostly right-wing elite project that is being championed by President Trump and the the many venture capitalists that are in his administration. And if you're already worried that it's going to take your job and you don't feel like you have any control over it, well, of course you're going to hate it.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: So I don't think this is some like elite right-wing plot. But it is definitely an elitist project that is being undertaken by a very small handful of people who are not elected, who don't have all that much accountability. Um, and I think that is in part by design. I think these people have seen that when you do give the public a right to weigh in on how technology is deployed, they mostly vote to stop it, right? As we're seeing now with these data centers, as we're seeing around the country with the backlash against Waymo, which we haven't even talked about, but like this has been truly surprising to me, is, you know, Waymo has now a technology in its self-driving cars that is demonstrably safer than human drivers, and you would think that that would be greeted as kind of an unambiguous good, and yet, you know, in a lot of places where they're trying to expand, people are saying, no, no, no, no, we, think of all the jobs we'll lose if this technology comes in.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: That attitude is mostly sort of mocked and dismissed as like, oh, you're like we're literally showing up here with a life-saving technology and you're saying what about the taxi drivers? And I think there's a cohort of people in Silicon Valley, many of whom we talk to and know, who just think like this technology is too important to be left to the masses. And I think that is like a misguided attitude, but it is definitely an attitude that is out there.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, I do think it is really misguided because it's one thing to say, well, we cannot trust the public to vote correctly about how new technologies could be deployed. It is another thing to actively fight against accountability measures or even transparency measures. And I just want to name this as another reason why I think that the public is growing increasingly anxious or even furious. You know, if you just look at OpenAI's lobbying efforts over the past couple of years, they lobbied against one of the first big AI transparency bills here in California and successfully killed it. Um, they have been sending subpoenas to people who work at nonprofits who were in favor of AI regulations, trying to insinuate that maybe they were the puppets of Elon Musk. That doesn't really feel like a very pro-democratic move. Most recently, OpenAI backed a bill in Illinois that would shield it from liability in cases where its models are used to cause serious harm, so long as they did not recklessly or intentionally cause it and published some safety reports. So to me, that is not just, hey, like let's embrace the spirit of permissionless innovation and see what kind of cool stuff we can do. It's saying, we've told you we're creating something that could be an existential risk to humanity and we're going to lobby for a bill that protect... that prevents us from being held liable. So to me, that is when I say this technology is elitist and anti-democratic, that is what I am talking about. They are fighting against the mechanisms of accountability and, you know, and so I understand why members of the public are upset about that.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I yeah, I don't think it's that simple. Like I think a lot of these companies have been, um, very open to regulation of some kind. Now, they have fought, you're right, they have fought specifically, um, some of these bills, but the people who are building this stuff do believe that it should be regulated. I don't think any of them think that it should be a total laissez-faire free-for-all. I think, you know, they just want there to be smart people making smart policies.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, we... there's... there's a perfect regulation out there and they can't seem to really name it or describe it or get it passed, but if they hear it someday, they promise they're going to line up behind it with their full support.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well, I want to just put a little bit of meat on that claim that I just made because they are actually proposing policies, right? The companies... So OpenAI last week released this

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: document called Industrial Policy for the Intelligent Age. It's sort of a white paper about some of their ideas for how policy and regulation might need to change in a world of very powerful AI systems. They say we should create a public wealth fund, similar to things that happen in Alaska for oil, where every citizen would get a stake in the up- economic upside of AI, improved safety nets for workers, establishing new public-private partnerships to accelerate energy production. Um, nothing like truly crazy, but it is just a slate of stuff that like I would be happy if one member of Congress was reading this and thinking this stuff looks like a good idea.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So I actually think it is pretty crazy. Like when you think about corporate policy papers that you've read in your time as a reporter, I'm guessing that you haven't read many that cause for a massive redistribution of wealth. That's essentially what this is. And when you look at what OpenAI is proposing and then you look at their political donations and lobbying, they just seem like they're at complete cross purposes, right? OpenAI is backing a lot of Republican candidates who I'm guessing are not going to support a massive expansion of the welfare state. So there- something is going on here that I think at the very least leave- leaves room for critics to say, are y'all even serious about this?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think some of what's going on right now at all of the leading AI companies is that they are trying to sort of plan for two worlds. One of them is a world of extreme acceleration in AI capabilities during the Trump term, right? Before 2028. And in that world, it really matters to have good relationships with Republican lawmakers and the White House. There's another world in which they are having to plan for a new president in 2029, and maybe that's a Democrat, maybe it's a Republican, but like maybe this stuff all takes until 2029 or so to get really crazy, and in that world, you actually want to start planting seeds with people in various different factions and coalitions. So like I think they are trying to kind of spread the bets a little bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and look, I- I will be the first to stipulate that like for the most part it should not be the job of the private corporations to like figure out how America should be governed. But we're in a situation where the government we have has been all too happy to take their lobbying dollars and then do almost whatever the- the companies are asking the government to do. And so that has just led to a world where again AI just looks like a top-down elitist project that the average person has no control over except in the one dimension where you always have control over American life, and that is in saying no to a project being built in your neighborhood. For some reason, this is the way- this is how we have decided to massively empower Americans is if there's anything that you don't want to see built, you probably actually can make that happen as an average citizen. So that's obviously very inspiring.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: ...ring, but I do wish we had other levers that we could pull.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah, in part because I don't think this is going to work, right? Like if you vote the the data center project out of your town, they're just going to go to another state or to Canada. They'll put the data centers in space, you know? They they've got options here, and I don't think this is going to meaningfully slow down or stop anything.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: I, when I hear you talk about this, my sense is that your real objection to the sort of like data center NIMBYism in particular is just that you think this technology is going to be really, really good for people. And you think people need to get out of the way and let the future happen.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: No, that is not what I'm saying at all. Like, I have real concerns about AI. I have real concerns about the kinds of job displacement that we may see as a result of this technology. But I think my concern is that people are identifying the wrong levers for change. Like, I do not think that stopping a data center from being built in your town has any marginal effect on the speed of AI progress or or the proliferation of AI throughout society. I think a lot of it is just that what you said is basically like true that people think that this is the area where they can actually change things, just as the thing that people thought they could do in the 1980s to block new construction in their neighborhoods was to like throw up a bunch of environmental reviews. Like, did that help the individual homeowners in that area who didn't want apartment buildings going up? Yes, it it kept their views unobstructed, but it also created a massive housing shortage in this state in particular that directly stems from this kind of NIMBY politics. And I just worry that the sort of data center NIMBYism that we're seeing will have some unforeseen consequences down the road.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Well, so then what are the right levers to pull, if you are worried about all of the harms and joblessness that AI seems likely to create?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: I think there are real policy proposals out there that people have been putting forward. One of them is actually in this OpenAI paper, which is for this kind of, you know, these more nimble and flexible social safety nets that could, for example, catch workers who become displaced by AI and pay for them to be retrained for some other skills. We saw things like this in the past when it came to manufacturing automation where in some countries they have these like job councils where, you know, you get laid off because a robot takes that job, but they sort of pay you for a period of time and retrain you to do something else so that you get to stay employed and and keep your standard of living while you're being displaced. Like that kind of thing feels like a better answer to me than just saying no data centers.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: It also seems to require all of like America to transform into Europe overnight, which seems somewhat unlikely to me. But inshallah, my friend.

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So what should the AI industry do? I mean, this is the question on a lot of people's minds right now is like what can they do?

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: to increase the public acceptance of or favorability toward the thing that they are building, or is this just kind of gonna be a project that they have to just push through regardless of public opposition?

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: So I really struggle here because I could tell you a bunch of messaging changes that they could make that might, you know, affect public perception of AI at the margins. This is not really a messaging challenge. The problem is not that they're talking about it in the wrong way. The problem is that they're saying that they are about to create a massive disruption in Americans' lives and they do not have a plan for what comes after that disruption. They have said your government is going to figure it out. And I think, particularly if you're an American right now and you're looking at the government that we have, it's very hard to believe that they're going to sort of adeptly navigate through that level of disruption. So I think, in short, Kevin, we have a governance problem, and that while the problem is being driven by the decisions of these, like, unelected AI leaders, it is ultimately the governments who are going to have to give us an answer to these questions.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Well, that's a punt. But—

full_transcript.speaker: Casey Newton
full_transcript.text: Is it though? It's a punt, but it feels like the only honest answer. Like, what, what am I supposed to say? Like, oh, well, you know, GPT-6 should blah, blah, blah. Like, come on.

full_transcript.speaker: Kevin Roose
full_transcript.text: Yeah. highlights: AstroForge is transitioning asteroid mining from science fiction to reality by developing spacecraft to prospect and extract platinum group metals from magnetic, near-Earth asteroids.

Political resistance to AI is intensifying through "data center NIMBYism" and legislative moratoriums, driven by public anxiety over job displacement, environmental impacts, and a lack of democratic oversight in tech development.

The "OpenNotes" project highlights a growing demand for open-source AI tools that allow users to run transcription and real-time fact-checking locally, prioritizing data privacy over third-party cloud processing.

High-budget streaming investments, such as the multi-billion-dollar Harry Potter series, are being driven by "lifetime value" economics where content acts as a loss leader for long-term subscriptions and theme park revenue.

Biohacking culture is facing criticism for focusing on expensive, "narcissistic" health stunts rather than substantive longevity drivers like universal healthcare, social connectivity, and gene-editing breakthroughs.
summary:
This episode of *This Week in Startups* explores the tension between frontier technology and its societal impact, ranging from asteroid mining to the growing public resistance against artificial intelligence. The show features Matt Galich, CEO of AstroForge, who details his company’s mission to extract platinum group metals from near-Earth asteroids, highlighting the shift of "science fiction into reality." However, the hosts contrast this technological optimism with a deep dive into the rising "AI NIMBYism," noting that many Americans are pushing for moratoriums on data centers. They discuss the disconnect between Silicon Valley elites and a public anxious about job displacement, environmental impacts, and a lack of democratic accountability in the AI industry.

The transcript also delves into the intersection of health, privacy, and software development. Iconic journalist Kara Swisher discusses her new CNN series on longevity, expressing skepticism toward the "narcissistic" biohacking trends of the tech elite while advocating for more accessible medical breakthroughs like CRISPR and universal healthcare. This theme of accessibility continues with a segment on "OpenNotes," an open-source AI tool designed for privacy-conscious meeting transcription. The creator, Yazın, demonstrates how AI models like Claude are lowering the barrier to software creation, allowing developers to build and maintain complex applications with minimal manual coding, signaling a potential "SaaS apocalypse" for traditional software companies.

Finally, the episode concludes with an "Off Duty" segment focused on the economics of major media franchises and personal productivity tools. The hosts analyze the massive financial investments in new *Lord of the Rings* and *Harry Potter* projects, arguing that the multi-billion dollar price tags are justified by the long-term subscription value and theme park synergies they generate. On a more personal note, Jason Calacanis highlights "Whisperflow" as a transformative AI dictation tool and discusses the utility of a USB foot pedal to streamline digital workflows. Throughout the conversation, a recurring theme emerges: while technology is advancing at an unprecedented pace, its success depends heavily on navigating complex human emotions, economic stability, and effective governance.
description:
This week, amid violent attacks on the homes of the OpenAI chief executive Sam Altman and the Indianapolis councilman Ron Gibson, we debate why artificial intelligence and data centers are so unpopular. Then, Kara Swisher returns to the show to discuss her new docuseries on Silicon Valley’s obsession with living longer. And finally, can chief executives replace themselves with A.I.? Mark Zuckerberg seems to be trying.

youtube_metadata.title: Why the A.I. Backlash Turned Violent in America
youtube_metadata.description:
This week, amid violent attacks on the homes of the OpenAI chief executive Sam Altman and the Indianapolis councilman Ron Gibson, we debate why artificial intelligence and data centers are so unpopular. Then, Kara Swisher returns to the show to discuss her new docuseries on Silicon Valley’s obsession with living longer. And finally, can chief executives replace themselves with A.I.? Mark Zuckerberg seems to be trying.

00:30 Sam Altman's House Attack and More A.I. Backlash
29:49 Kara Swisher Doesn't Actually Want to Live Forever
49:19 Mark Zuckerberg's A.I. Bot

Guest:
Kara Swisher, tech journalist and host of the podcasts “Pivot” and “On With Kara Swisher”
